title: Nolan's Odyssey - A very technical analysis by a very nitpicky Classics student - Earl Rambles

content_html:
PREFACE

This is not a be-all-end-all review, this is a more critical analysis of the choices made concerning the writing and plot from the lens of criticising an adaptation. There were bits I liked about this film, but I believe some of the choices made detract from the original themes and characters from the text, to the extents that they are genuinely unfaithful at points. Not saying it makes the film unwatchable or awful, but saying that at times, it doesn’t feel like the Odyssey. But hey. Classics students amiright. I will not cover everything in this, because my memory is not good enough for that, but I’ll include as much as I can. This is very stream of consciousness, so I apologise in advance if this makes no sense. Happy to clarify anything that is incoherent lol. SPOILERS ARE ABOUND. IF YOU WANT AN UNSPOILED EXPERIENCE READ OR WATCH FIRST. Ok enjoy.

ODYSSEUS IS NOT ODYSSEUS

  • They take out all his tricks!!! No overcoming the Cyclops through trickery, no deceiving Circe! Guy whose main epithets (and intro in opening lines) is as a trickster and he does no tricks!!!
  • He is essentially written as Achilles, inserted into Odysseus’ plot. Very puritan action hero who beats everyone up and challenges everyone and surges into unsafe situations without scouting. I find him to be a rather Americanised, war-guilty action hero (á la Iraq or Vietnam related films), a modernisation which strays a little too far from Odysseus as a character for my liking.
  • (NB, read a review stating that Nolan seems to have actually wanted to adapt the Odyssey which. Actually makes a lot of sense. But it wasn’t in my initial analysis so I will link it here)
  • Guy whose main driving force is wanting to go home literally says the line “maybe I don’t want to go home.” Insane work. He literally spends 7 years MISERABLE on Calypso’s island weeping and begging to go home and instead he just. Forgets. With the lotus (which shouldn’t be on Ogygia). For plot. Sure. (I assume this was an attempt to explain why he was stuck on Ogygia for so long which to be fair is hard to do, so I get that. Even more so when you remove the divine, but we will get to that later :)) )
  • They also try to make him a perfect action hero and detract from all of his morally grey moments - his second in command Eurylochus randomly makes some of Odysseus’ bad decisions seemingly taking away his agency in making those choices which end up harming his men - at Scylla and with Circe (random mutiny very early on?? Is Odysseus that bad a commander??) Imo too much “action hero”-ing and not enough fighting with creativity and intelligence - he’s not favoured by Athena for nothing!! (or not at all, ig??)
  • Weirdly seems to be a bad leader??? Like seems to make no decisions, gets mutinied so quickly and for little discernible reason. His men do not trust him or seem to respect him at all. Idk just a bit strange.
  • His trickery at the end also is very watered down. Lack of divine influence results in it just being Matt Damon in a hood rather than an actual disguise.
  • I thought the actual suitor slaughter was actually a little underwhelming? Action hero-ification of Odysseus does not extend to his ability to kill the suitors ig? Again, issue of not having gods (Zeus and Athena help in text, as does Telemachus instead of running around being kind of useless and being told to stay out of it). Severe lack of ruthlessness from Odysseus. Missing the driving force of wanting to return home no matter the cost, no matter the sacrifice. I felt leaving some of the suitors who surrendered alive really detracted from the visceral catharsis and kind of undermined Odysseus’ authority? Like yes these men pursued your wife for years and mocked and intimidated your son and ruined your household but at least they said sorry! The purification of kind of morally grey heroes frustrates me.

NO GODS

  • This is dumb for many reasons. Taking a story driven and incited by actions of gods and removing any real evidence of them doesn’t really work, because it then removes credibility from multiple parts of the story. 
  • Nolan wanting to show forces of nature as gods is fun, but ineffective when you don’t actually explain that to the audience as a direct cause!! A one-off mention to Poseidon is hard for those who are unfamiliar with Greek myths to understand! Even more so when the next storm is actually sent by a different god! (Storm after Helios’ island is sent by Zeus. This is not well communicated) 
  • Most obvious with the Cyclops, lack of direct and overt address to Poseidon from Polyphemus praying for revenge and for Odysseus to never make it home makes the Greeks seem incompetent in their travels!! The whole point is that no matter how good they are and what they do, because of the insult to Poseidon they will never make it home safely!! They are doomed by Odysseus and are forced to foot his punishment!! Removal of gods removes the interplay between fate and free will, and the notions of godly politics influencing how gods and mortals interact 
  • Additionally, removal of gods forces wisdom imparted by gods (normal, believable as all seeing immortals to be able to act as messengers in this way) to be imparted by random characters. why is Circe randomly giving prophetic advice? Basically, it makes a lot of the exposition seem more forced than it should be.
  • No Hermes :( crime in and of itself. 
  • Also, a lot of the characters whom Athena is disguised as in the text were actually in the film! Everyone Telemachus tells that they have ‘wise eyes. Athena eyes’ is meant to be Athena in the text, but obviously NOT meant to be given they kill Mentor during the assassination. I’m so sad we didn’t get Hermes.
  • The Athena thing was interesting I actually liked that the image taken was that of a girl from Troy, i think the implication is that Odysseus was just hallucinating her?? and she isn’t actually the goddess. She didn’t feel very impactful as a character, though i suppose she’s technically meant to serve as a representation of Odysseus’ guilty conscience. Which again, don’t hate it, but frustrating to build up this concept of Odysseus as a tormented morally pure hero rather than a deceitful trickster willing to do whatever it takes to get home. Idk.
  • As a result of lack of divine, try to make excuses for other reasons for Trojan war which, understand why he’s done that, but when the entirety of the war on multiple levels and its perpetuity hinges on the interference and politics of the divines it’s a bit weird. mentions about trade routes and mortal politics to replace the divine, but also inciting incident of the Trojan war is literally bc of gods. Are we ignoring that? Do we care? 
  • Cherry picking of the divine. Not wanting to actually put in the gods but wanting their effects to come into play without effective communication to audience imo. Like we have Helios’ island (which, did they call him Apollo? I can’t remember but I feel like I crashed out at that point for some reason) which is obviously divine, and there is divine retribution for disobeying the gods, but that comes from Zeus due to divine politics, a layer of vetting of divine punishment that is lost due to lack of direct communication to audience about the gods.
  • ADDITIONALLY, the whole point of an epic is that the poet is a vessel for the divine. Mortals cannot know the story, so they exhort to the Muses and channel their speech in recounting it. Epics are inherently divine! Why are we scared of leaning into this! Had the suggestion that this was an atheist take on the Odyssey coming from the perspective of reading it with the exclusion of the divine and trying to find the source of the actions which are explained by the divine by the Greeks. I agree this is likely the case, but I feel it’s a disservice to the story to exclude such a major part. That’s not just a differing interpretation, it’s an ignorant at best and wilful at worst exclusion of a massive influence on the plot and the themes imo.
  • Crash course in the Greek hospitality rules!! ‘ξενια’ - “xenia” – the concept that all mortals should offer help to strangers who come to their homes - food, bathing, a bed for the night - as they could be a god in disguise. As a result, the Greek word for “stranger” – ‘ξενος’ – “xenos”, can also be translated as “guest-friend”. A major theme of the Odyssey is that of hospitality and its violations, and the consequences of such things. 
  • this is what they referred to as Zeus’ law. It is not actually called that. And they just name dropped it 200000 times without actually taking the time to investigate what it means in context and explain how its violation is significant. Treat others how you want to be treated is a shallow interpretation, and genuine questioning of what it means to give, receive and violate xenia were omitted from a lot of the journey interactions, where these themes hold significance – violations from the Greeks and altogether rejection with Polyphemus, deceitful violations with Circe, overprovision with Calypso, dangers of provision for the Phaeacians. Felt like a very surface level interaction with an absolutely core theme to me

Break it down sectional style

  • ok so I understand cutting things for time, I am ok with that. my issues were somewhat with what and how things were cut and the amount of attention and time given to certain parts. 
  • I thought the pacing was really weird during the journey sequence. I don’t know why they rushed scenes like the cyclops and Circe, yet gave so much time to other, less significant moments, or additions that don’t necessarily forward the story or the themes.
  • Begins with Telemachus on Ithaca - personally thought this bit went on for too long and without the textual inciting incident (Athena speaking to Telemachus in disguise) it dragged a bit, and Telemachus’ decision to actually do something came a little out of nowhere. I’d have liked less of that at the start (or for it to have been more succinct) and more of the actual journey. Liked the suitors tho!! 
  • No Nestor!!! My goat I’m so sorry they skipped you.
  • THEY SKIPPED HELEN DRUGGING EVERYONE AND I’M MAD ABOUT IT. Liked Helen, liked how vindicative she was, would have liked to see a more complicated, survivalist, playing-each-side vibe to her rather than seeming to just be angry with and hate her husband and her husband hate her. mixed opinions about Menelaus for reasons I can’t really explain. Vibes I guess.
  • No Phaeacians :(( In-text, Odysseus washes onshore at Scheria and is taken in by the Phaeacians - initial example of good hospitality to contrast the suitors. Their absence messes with the structure of the story quite a lot, and ruins some of the lovely ring composition :( He tells them the story, not calypso, and they then help him get home and are punished by Poseidon for doing so. (Q of conflict between Xenia and the direct will of the gods). Disregarding the divine makes it hard to include them, but I think they are a more meaningful audience than Calypso - mortal audience rep of the actual listeners of the epic.  
  • No Aeolus!! The floating island!! :( again, cut for time and for not having gods but that bit is fun :(
  • Calypso - why are we there for so long!! Imo too much time spent there. Also why are there lotus flowers there? Interesting approach, tying into what I believe is the mischaracterisation of Odysseus in avoiding returning home (lotus makes you forget friends and family). Liked the costuming for calypso, would have liked a more interesting look for the island (felt kinda grey and barren to me)
  • Polyphemus gets his own section later.
  • I liked the Laestrygonians!!! Was lowkey surprised they made it in so props. Maybe too much time spent there though. Big battle scene necessary I suppose. 
  • WHY were Circe and the Laestrygonians on the same island. what. This is insane work. Weird lack of focus and time spent on the actual locations of the journey. No lotus eaters either.
  • Circe was so cool. The transformation was actually sickening. I would have liked to see more of her and have her be more obviously powerful like in the text. Sad we didn’t get her palaces as she deserved, and would’ve loved more time and intrigue and actual trickery wars between her and Odysseus (the plight of no gods. No Hermes = no moly = no being able to dramatic irony your way through the poisoning. Tragic)
  • loved the sirens tbh. Very fun.
  • Helios’ island was good actually. Again lacking in the more overt divine aspects but that’s to be expected now.
  • I LOVED the underworld, what a fun way to do that. Them crawling up out of the ground was so peak, covered in the black dirt. Amaze amaze amaze. Sad that Odysseus’ mother was excluded, that’s a lovely moment of grief as he realises that his actions and hubris has led to more than the deaths of his crew, but has also led directly to personal loss and the suffering of his loved ones, the first tangible recognition of this.
  • On that note, Laertes is just. Not in the film at all. Which is a choice I guess. Final moment of homecoming is the reunion between Odysseus and his father, which is just. Absent. Again, really feels like the homecoming theme is sidelined, which, given that’s one of the core themes, maybe an oversight?
  • Very much enjoyed the sacking of troy, this is actually my favourite interpretation of the Trojan horse I’ve seen as well, LOVED the half-buried in sand vibe and the literal drowning with the tide. Based. Whole invasion sequence was also fun. Liked the detail of the Trojans using the rolling logs to move it too. HOWEVER. For a film whose new and added theme is the horror of war, it felt very unhorrible? The barbarism of the Greeks at times felt watered down, and I felt Nolan shied away from the true crimes that were committed (assaults, taking slaves etc) which I think ties back to this puritan image of a troubled soldier that he wants Odysseus to represent. YES, he’s TROUBLED by WAR but he didn‘t do too many bad things so he’s redeemable!! Odysseus is kind of a bastard in the text and everyone knows it! Lean into that and I assure you you’ll get better internal conflict than “I don’t want to go home” from guy whose entire purpose in this story is seeking home.
  • There is lots of boat! This is good and bad! I like how the boats look and sail! But maybe too much boat at times. Could have done with like. 25% less boat.

POLYPHEMUS. Oh my god. How do you fumble the best part of the text. 

  • absolute lack of tricks!! This is Odysseus’ time to shine and nothing happened!! Where was my arrogant attempts to debate Polyphemus? Where was my using the wine to knock him out (example of violating Xenia and trying to impart Greek philosophies on foreign peoples) to Where was my “I am Noman”? Where was my hiding under the sheep! Tragic. Core part of Odysseus’ character is just missing. ALSO I WANTED THE BIG SHEEP :(
  • Making Polyphemus an ineloquent/unintelligent creature rather than a speaking, divine adjacent being undermines that whole fight imo. It’s man vs monster not man vs enemy (if that makes sense??) Odysseus doesn’t have to outsmart an intelligent being, but a more animalistic creature. Taking away that intelligence reduces him to another monster of the week instead of the opponent who understands Odysseus’ intentions and offer of peace and instead makes the explicit choice of violence - shows a different sort of danger. Asking Odysseus’ name in what seems to be an acceptance of hospitality then turning around to state that he will kill him last. Absolute cinema.
  • Additionally, losing the speech results in no direct prayer to Poseidon (additional issue of lack of direct divinity) – as a result, 1 line of exposition revealing Polyphemus as son of Poseidon and a vague implication that this will be bad. Direct prayer is so key to original as it is the moment that Odysseus’ arrogance, intelligence and hubris dooms himself and his crew to their fates. His need to outsmart and gloat over his opponent causes Polyphemus to call directly for retribution, praying that Odysseus will never make it home, or that if he does he will be late, without honour and following the death of his whole crew. Key moment of damnation becomes vague and unclear, the tragic doomed narrative is lost as we don’t have the divine confirmation that every attempt Odysseus makes to fight fate and save his crew is in vain. Small details have important narrative consequence.
  • The look of the cyclops was excellent though, I was very freaked out by him. Apparently he was part puppetry too!
  • Scylla was so cool. Genuine jumpscare moment and I knew it was coming! Not much else on that tbh, quite a short section. My brother did point out that there did seem to be a lot of empty space next to Charybdis tho which like. Bit less believable that they couldn’t just. Sail around her. I’ll give it a pass though. (Also fun fact this is where the phrase ‘between a rock and a hard place’ comes from)
  •   I liked the Sinon addition actually! Fun replacement for Elpenor’s role in the underworld and gives some fun surrounding context to the Trojan horse. Based. Liked the excuse for extra suffering for Antinous too. 
  • I love Tiresias. That’s it that’s the bullet point.
  • Why was there an assassination attempt at Pylos? IMO wasting time that could be used more productively on textual (or text expansion) parts to have another pointless fight scene. Quite a lot of pointless fight scenes. And quite a lot of being chased by armies back to the ship. It’s almost a running gag at this point. Don’t hate that bit tbh.
  • I have opinions about Nolan’s writing of female characters. You’ve given calypso more screen time only for her to just. Prompt Odysseus. Why. Use that time to investigate godhood and its (in)compatibility with mortality. Or something more than being a prompter for a man. Idk. I would’ve liked to see more of Circe being powerful and manipulative and vicious towards the men (yes, even more than we got) and less of Circe immediately folding to Odysseus after he asks nicely. TRICKERY AND DECEIT. Make her stronger willed!! Circe was generally decent tho. Women felt generally quite one note. This leads us to:

  Penelope misrepresentation :((

  • I felt very much that the majority of trying to make “strong female characters” resulted in making Penelope be angry and shout and hit us with classic lines like “women can’t take the throne!!” Which, yes, point. However, that’s not really how Penelope approached it?? Penelope and Odysseus work because they are equally as tricksy and intelligent as each other. They brought in the weaving trick (which in the text is already concluded) so I would’ve loved to see that represented rather than expositioned at us! Show me Penelope weaving all day, then tirelessly unweaving it by candlelight all night. Show me her plotting more subtle ways to delay the suitors instead of just shouting empty words! I don’t mind extrapolation to improve a character, but align it with their actual characterisation!
  • And to boot, we absolutely ignore the best scene for Penelope which proves her intellectual equality to Odysseus - the olive bed scene!!! Post suitor slaughter, Penelope does not trust that this man is Odysseus (the pin does not exist. Done for cinematic ease I presume. Maybe if we didn’t spend so much time on boats we’d have had time for the DEFINING MOMENT OF THEIR RELATIONSHIP IN THE TEXT.) She tells him to move their bed in their room, a final test for Odysseus to prove himself, as their wedding bed was carved by Odysseus, with one post being a live olive tree – room built around the tree. Him pointing this out proves his identity, and shows Penelope’s loyalty, dedication and intellect at the same time, withholding her relief until she is absolutely certain her husband is home.
  • I appreciate attempts to give Penelope more agency but her entire arc is founded on her inability to take direct action. Subtle actions are her strength, and some elements which give thematic and societal information are also excluded – the veil is one I noticed (in some ways replaced by the screen wall and door, but lacks the societal implications). High born women wore veils when in public and had to be accompanied by chaperones – the interesting part is that Penelope begins to wear a veil in her house when speaking to the suitors, and be accompanied by two of her maids at all times – implication is that the invasion of the suitors has destroyed the notion of home for Penelope, forcing her to take outside precautions in her own household.
  • Theme of home and homecoming and what it means to come home is absolutely key to this story, so this implication that despite the house still standing, the home has been destroyed by Odysseus’ absence and the imposition of the suitors is less prominent as a result of the omission of these details.
  • For Odysseus, this is the question of: when am I home? Is it arriving back on Ithaca? Is it finally seeing his son? Is it reclaiming his house? Is it finally reuniting with Penelope? Is it seeing his father again after so long? Home and homecoming is core to Odysseus’ motivations, and the way that homes are built and honoured and violated and destroyed is key to this text.
  • This theme ties into xenia and customs concerning appropriate behaviour as guests and towards guests when entering homes. I also didn’t love the ending for this reason. What do you mean Odysseus spends that long trying to come home then IMMEDIATELY LEAVES HIS HOME AND HIS SON BEHIND. WHY. This is his son, whom he pretended to be senile in order to try and see grow up and avoid fighting at troy. And you expect me to believe he’s just going to leave him behind?
  • I know costumes and sets were inaccurate, but that’s not my department so I won’t comment too much. General notes were that at times it was pulled off, at others it felt like bad cosplay and very hollywoodified. Would have loved to see accurate armour and actually create a new aesthetic identity rather than submitting to the stereotyped roman hollywoodified look but oh well. Agamemnon’s Darth Vader horse armour lowkey cooked though. 
  • I LOVED THE MUSIC I LOVED THE SCORE I thought it was absolutely gorgeous. Huge props. Loved the slight marry of musicality with Odysseus and him strumming the bowstring too that was a nice touch.

TO CONCLUDE

Some of this is nitpicky, some of it, I believe, is not and is a genuine criticism coming from the POV of someone very familiar with the text. Yes I understand things had to be changed for film format and time, no I don’t think that is an excuse for some of the choices made – the mischaracterisation of Odysseus especially, and I disagree with Nolan’s approach to the divine as I think the absence of the divine forces cuts and rearrangements that overcomplicate and detract from key plot points and themes. It’s clear that Nolan had themes he wanted to insert in (his classic lonely tormented man, impacts of war on the soldier and the world and its inhabitants), and while that is present in og Odyssey, the text has far more diverse and complex themes which take precedence over those. I find it a little frustrating when the core message of a text is sidelined to the extent that characters, and even the story is changed, under the guise and good PR of a famous (but culturally significant and semantically loaded) source material. This is certainly a story inspired by the Odyssey, but it is not a wholly faithful adaptation and that should be communicated to the audience. It undervalues the core characteristics of its protagonist and supporting characters and fails to explore the rich culture of the divine and mythological surroundings upon which the roots of the source text are built on. It is the interaction and dissonance between the divine and the mortal which makes the text so fascinating, the interplay between fate and free will and the impossibility of success and safety in a world where gods can damn you with one word. Actions have consequences, and the divine are always prepared to serve retribution.

I believe that this being a Hollywood film and needing to be accessible to a wider audience should not result in the omission of and dumbing down of cultural concepts (see Xenia > Zeus’ law), but I would have loved a meaningful investigation into the culture and societal struggles of the time, which by imposing his own themes and ignoring those of the text, Nolan fails to achieve.

AGAIN, this is all my opinion and coming from a highly critical perspective. The film was pretty enjoyable (if REALLY loud. like. ow.) and there were lots of choices I liked!! But I am burdened with glorious purpose (and 2/3 of a classics degree) so I am required to be very analytical. This is a text that means a lot to a lot of people and holds massive cultural significance, so to wholly change parts of the core themes and meanings I feel is unnecessary. There are so many wonderful Greek plays about many of the themes Nolan imposes, so I always wonder in scenarios like this why he didn’t take one more suited. Imposing his own main themes with the result that they actively contradict with core part of the story, I feel, does not result in a good adaptation! (All my opinion I must remind)

AGAIN AGAIN, there was a lot about this that I enjoyed, I am delighted that so many people are taking an interest and I sincerely hope that anyone who has watched it and is interested (and if you’ve made it this far through this stream of consciousness), goes and reads the text for themselves! It’s available online for free here on the Project Gutenberg website. Go wild, form your own opinions, and debate. I hope this is coherent.

AND, keep reading classical literature!! :D

Additional recommendations (AKA Greek plays I like that you should read while Odyssey mania persists):

Oedipus Tyrannus – Sophocles

Antigone - Sophocles

Elektra – Euripides

The Women of Troy – Euripides

Agamemnon – Aeschylus

Aeneid – Virgil (Latin but the founding story of Rome building off of Homer’s Odyssey following the surviving Trojans on their trip to Troy. A merging of the Iliad and Odyssey with Roman setting)

ALSO read the Iliad. Duh.

Ok bye.

As a result of lack of divine, try to make excuses for other reasons for Trojan war which, understand why he’s done that, but when the entirety of the war on multiple levels and its perpetuity hinges on the interference and politics of the divines it’s a bit weird. mentions about trade routes and mortal politics to replace the divine, but also inciting incident of the Trojan war is literally bc of gods. Are we ignoring that? Do we care? Cherry picking of the divine. Not wanting to actually put in the gods but wanting their effects to come into play without effective communication to audience imo. Like we have Helios’ island (which, did they call him Apollo? I can’t remember but I feel like I crashed out at that point for some reason) which is obviously divine, and there is divine retribution for disobeying the gods, but that comes from Zeus due to divine politics, a layer of vetting of divine punishment that is lost due to lack of direct communication to audience about the gods. ADDITIONALLY, the whole point of an epic is that the poet is a vessel for the divine. Mortals cannot know the story, so they exhort to the Muses and channel their speech in recounting it. Epics are inherently divine! Why are we scared of leaning into this! Had the suggestion that this was an atheist take on the Odyssey coming from the perspective of reading it with the exclusion of the divine and trying to find the source of the actions which are explained by the divine by the Greeks. I agree this is likely the case, but I feel it’s a disservice to the story to exclude such a major part. That’s not just a differing interpretation, it’s an ignorant at best and wilful at worst exclusion of a massive influence on the plot and the themes imo. Crash course in the Greek hospitality rules!! ‘ξενια’ - “xenia” – the concept that all mortals should offer help to strangers who come to their homes - food, bathing, a bed for the night - as they could be a god in disguise. As a result, the Greek word for “stranger” – ‘ξενος’ – “xenos”, can also be translated as “guest-friend”. A major theme of the Odyssey is that of hospitality and its violations, and the consequences of such things. this is what they referred to as Zeus’ law. It is not actually called that. And they just name dropped it 200000 times without actually taking the time to investigate what it means in context and explain how its violation is significant. Treat others how you want to be treated is a shallow interpretation, and genuine questioning of what it means to give, receive and violate xenia were omitted from a lot of the journey interactions, where these themes hold significance – violations from the Greeks and altogether rejection with Polyphemus, deceitful violations with Circe, overprovision with Calypso, dangers of provision for the Phaeacians. Felt like a very surface level interaction with an absolutely core theme to me Break it down sectional style ok so I understand cutting things for time, I am ok with that. my issues were somewhat with what and how things were cut and the amount of attention and time given to certain parts. I thought the pacing was really weird during the journey sequence. I don’t know why they rushed scenes like the cyclops and Circe, yet gave so much time to other, less significant moments, or additions that don’t necessarily forward the story or the themes. Begins with Telemachus on Ithaca - personally thought this bit went on for too long and without the textual inciting incident (Athena speaking to Telemachus in disguise) it dragged a bit, and Telemachus’ decision to actually do something came a little out of nowhere. I’d have liked less of that at the start (or for it to have been more succinct) and more of the actual journey. Liked the suitors tho!! No Nestor!!! My goat I’m so sorry they skipped you. THEY SKIPPED HELEN DRUGGING EVERYONE AND I’M MAD ABOUT IT. Liked Helen, liked how vindicative she was, would have liked to see a more complicated, survivalist, playing-each-side vibe to her rather than seeming to just be angry with and hate her husband and her husband hate her. mixed opinions about Menelaus for reasons I can’t really explain. Vibes I guess. No Phaeacians :(( In-text, Odysseus washes onshore at Scheria and is taken in by the Phaeacians - initial example of good hospitality to contrast the suitors. Their absence messes with the structure of the story quite a lot, and ruins some of the lovely ring composition :( He tells them the story, not calypso, and they then help him get home and are punished by Poseidon for doing so. (Q of conflict between Xenia and the direct will of the gods). Disregarding the divine makes it hard to include them, but I think they are a more meaningful audience than Calypso - mortal audience rep of the actual listeners of the epic. No Aeolus!! The floating island!! :( again, cut for time and for not having gods but that bit is fun :( Calypso - why are we there for so long!! Imo too much time spent there. Also why are there lotus flowers there? Interesting approach, tying into what I believe is the mischaracterisation of Odysseus in avoiding returning home (lotus makes you forget friends and family). Liked the costuming for calypso, would have liked a more interesting look for the island (felt kinda grey and barren to me) Polyphemus gets his own section later. I liked the Laestrygonians!!! Was lowkey surprised they made it in so props. Maybe too much time spent there though. Big battle scene necessary I suppose. WHY were Circe and the Laestrygonians on the same island. what. This is insane work. Weird lack of focus and time spent on the actual locations of the journey. No lotus eaters either. Circe was so cool. The transformation was actually sickening. I would have liked to see more of her and have her be more obviously powerful like in the text. Sad we didn’t get her palaces as she deserved, and would’ve loved more time and intrigue and actual trickery wars between her and Odysseus (the plight of no gods. No Hermes = no moly = no being able to dramatic irony your way through the poisoning. Tragic) loved the sirens tbh. Very fun. Helios’ island was good actually. Again lacking in the more overt divine aspects but that’s to be expected now. I LOVED the underworld, what a fun way to do that. Them crawling up out of the ground was so peak, covered in the black dirt. Amaze amaze amaze. Sad that Odysseus’ mother was excluded, that’s a lovely moment of grief as he realises that his actions and hubris has led to more than the deaths of his crew, but has also led directly to personal loss and the suffering of his loved ones, the first tangible recognition of this. On that note, Laertes is just. Not in the film at all. Which is a choice I guess. Final moment of homecoming is the reunion between Odysseus and his father, which is just. Absent. Again, really feels like the homecoming theme is sidelined, which, given that’s one of the core themes, maybe an oversight? Very much enjoyed the sacking of troy, this is actually my favourite interpretation of the Trojan horse I’ve seen as well, LOVED the half-buried in sand vibe and the literal drowning with the tide. Based. Whole invasion sequence was also fun. Liked the detail of the Trojans using the rolling logs to move it too. HOWEVER. For a film whose new and added theme is the horror of war, it felt very unhorrible? The barbarism of the Greeks at times felt watered down, and I felt Nolan shied away from the true crimes that were committed (assaults, taking slaves etc) which I think ties back to this puritan image of a troubled soldier that he wants Odysseus to represent. YES, he’s TROUBLED by WAR but he didn‘t do too many bad things so he’s redeemable!! Odysseus is kind of a bastard in the text and everyone knows it! Lean into that and I assure you you’ll get better internal conflict than “I don’t want to go home” from guy whose entire purpose in this story is seeking home. There is lots of boat! This is good and bad! I like how the boats look and sail! But maybe too much boat at times. Could have done with like. 25% less boat.POLYPHEMUS. Oh my god. How do you fumble the best part of the text. absolute lack of tricks!! This is Odysseus’ time to shine and nothing happened!! Where was my arrogant attempts to debate Polyphemus? Where was my using the wine to knock him out (example of violating Xenia and trying to impart Greek philosophies on foreign peoples) to Where was my “I am Noman”? Where was my hiding under the sheep! Tragic. Core part of Odysseus’ character is just missing. ALSO I WANTED THE BIG SHEEP :( Making Polyphemus an ineloquent/unintelligent creature rather than a speaking, divine adjacent being undermines that whole fight imo. It’s man vs monster not man vs enemy (if that makes sense??) Odysseus doesn’t have to outsmart an intelligent being, but a more animalistic creature. Taking away that intelligence reduces him to another monster of the week instead of the opponent who understands Odysseus’ intentions and offer of peace and instead makes the explicit choice of violence - shows a different sort of danger. Asking Odysseus’ name in what seems to be an acceptance of hospitality then turning around to state that he will kill him last. Absolute cinema. Additionally, losing the speech results in no direct prayer to Poseidon (additional issue of lack of direct divinity) – as a result, 1 line of exposition revealing Polyphemus as son of Poseidon and a vague implication that this will be bad. Direct prayer is so key to original as it is the moment that Odysseus’ arrogance, intelligence and hubris dooms himself and his crew to their fates. His need to outsmart and gloat over his opponent causes Polyphemus to call directly for retribution, praying that Odysseus will never make it home, or that if he does he will be late, without honour and following the death of his whole crew. Key moment of damnation becomes vague and unclear, the tragic doomed narrative is lost as we don’t have the divine confirmation that every attempt Odysseus makes to fight fate and save his crew is in vain. Small details have important narrative consequence. The look of the cyclops was excellent though, I was very freaked out by him. Apparently he was part puppetry too! Scylla was so cool. Genuine jumpscare moment and I knew it was coming! Not much else on that tbh, quite a short section. My brother did point out that there did seem to be a lot of empty space next to Charybdis tho which like. Bit less believable that they couldn’t just. Sail around her. I’ll give it a pass though. (Also fun fact this is where the phrase ‘between a rock and a hard place’ comes from) I liked the Sinon addition actually! Fun replacement for Elpenor’s role in the underworld and gives some fun surrounding context to the Trojan horse. Based. Liked the excuse for extra suffering for Antinous too. I love Tiresias. That’s it that’s the bullet point. Why was there an assassination attempt at Pylos? IMO wasting time that could be used more productively on textual (or text expansion) parts to have another pointless fight scene. Quite a lot of pointless fight scenes. And quite a lot of being chased by armies back to the ship. It’s almost a running gag at this point. Don’t hate that bit tbh.I have opinions about Nolan’s writing of female characters. You’ve given calypso more screen time only for her to just. Prompt Odysseus. Why. Use that time to investigate godhood and its (in)compatibility with mortality. Or something more than being a prompter for a man. Idk. I would’ve liked to see more of Circe being powerful and manipulative and vicious towards the men (yes, even more than we got) and less of Circe immediately folding to Odysseus after he asks nicely. TRICKERY AND DECEIT. Make her stronger willed!! Circe was generally decent tho. Women felt generally quite one note. This leads us to: Penelope misrepresentation :(( I felt very much that the majority of trying to make “strong female characters” resulted in making Penelope be angry and shout and hit us with classic lines like “women can’t take the throne!!” Which, yes, point. However, that’s not really how Penelope approached it?? Penelope and Odysseus work because they are equally as tricksy and intelligent as each other. They brought in the weaving trick (which in the text is already concluded) so I would’ve loved to see that represented rather than expositioned at us! Show me Penelope weaving all day, then tirelessly unweaving it by candlelight all night. Show me her plotting more subtle ways to delay the suitors instead of just shouting empty words! I don’t mind extrapolation to improve a character, but align it with their actual characterisation! And to boot, we absolutely ignore the best scene for Penelope which proves her intellectual equality to Odysseus - the olive bed scene!!! Post suitor slaughter, Penelope does not trust that this man is Odysseus (the pin does not exist. Done for cinematic ease I presume. Maybe if we didn’t spend so much time on boats we’d have had time for the DEFINING MOMENT OF THEIR RELATIONSHIP IN THE TEXT.) She tells him to move their bed in their room, a final test for Odysseus to prove himself, as their wedding bed was carved by Odysseus, with one post being a live olive tree – room built around the tree. Him pointing this out proves his identity, and shows Penelope’s loyalty, dedication and intellect at the same time, withholding her relief until she is absolutely certain her husband is home. I appreciate attempts to give Penelope more agency but her entire arc is founded on her inability to take direct action. Subtle actions are her strength, and some elements which give thematic and societal information are also excluded – the veil is one I noticed (in some ways replaced by the screen wall and door, but lacks the societal implications). High born women wore veils when in public and had to be accompanied by chaperones – the interesting part is that Penelope begins to wear a veil in her house when speaking to the suitors, and be accompanied by two of her maids at all times – implication is that the invasion of the suitors has destroyed the notion of home for Penelope, forcing her to take outside precautions in her own household. Theme of home and homecoming and what it means to come home is absolutely key to this story, so this implication that despite the house still standing, the home has been destroyed by Odysseus’ absence and the imposition of the suitors is less prominent as a result of the omission of these details. For Odysseus, this is the question of: when am I home? Is it arriving back on Ithaca? Is it finally seeing his son? Is it reclaiming his house? Is it finally reuniting with Penelope? Is it seeing his father again after so long? Home and homecoming is core to Odysseus’ motivations, and the way that homes are built and honoured and violated and destroyed is key to this text. This theme ties into xenia and customs concerning appropriate behaviour as guests and towards guests when entering homes. I also didn’t love the ending for this reason. What do you mean Odysseus spends that long trying to come home then IMMEDIATELY LEAVES HIS HOME AND HIS SON BEHIND. WHY. This is his son, whom he pretended to be senile in order to try and see grow up and avoid fighting at troy. And you expect me to believe he’s just going to leave him behind? I know costumes and sets were inaccurate, but that’s not my department so I won’t comment too much. General notes were that at times it was pulled off, at others it felt like bad cosplay and very hollywoodified. Would have loved to see accurate armour and actually create a new aesthetic identity rather than submitting to the stereotyped roman hollywoodified look but oh well. Agamemnon’s Darth Vader horse armour lowkey cooked though. I LOVED THE MUSIC I LOVED THE SCORE I thought it was absolutely gorgeous. Huge props. Loved the slight marry of musicality with Odysseus and him strumming the bowstring too that was a nice touch. TO CONCLUDESome of this is nitpicky, some of it, I believe, is not and is a genuine criticism coming from the POV of someone very familiar with the text. Yes I understand things had to be changed for film format and time, no I don’t think that is an excuse for some of the choices made – the mischaracterisation of Odysseus especially, and I disagree with Nolan’s approach to the divine as I think the absence of the divine forces cuts and rearrangements that overcomplicate and detract from key plot points and themes. It’s clear that Nolan had themes he wanted to insert in (his classic lonely tormented man, impacts of war on the soldier and the world and its inhabitants), and while that is present in og Odyssey, the text has far more diverse and complex themes which take precedence over those. I find it a little frustrating when the core message of a text is sidelined to the extent that characters, and even the story is changed, under the guise and good PR of a famous (but culturally significant and semantically loaded) source material. This is certainly a story inspired by the Odyssey, but it is not a wholly faithful adaptation and that should be communicated to the audience. It undervalues the core characteristics of its protagonist and supporting characters and fails to explore the rich culture of the divine and mythological surroundings upon which the roots of the source text are built on. It is the interaction and dissonance between the divine and the mortal which makes the text so fascinating, the interplay between fate and free will and the impossibility of success and safety in a world where gods can damn you with one word. Actions have consequences, and the divine are always prepared to serve retribution.I believe that this being a Hollywood film and needing to be accessible to a wider audience should not result in the omission of and dumbing down of cultural concepts (see Xenia > Zeus’ law), but I would have loved a meaningful investigation into the culture and societal struggles of the time, which by imposing his own themes and ignoring those of the text, Nolan fails to achieve.AGAIN, this is all my opinion and coming from a highly critical perspective. The film was pretty enjoyable (if REALLY loud. like. ow.) and there were lots of choices I liked!! But I am burdened with glorious purpose (and 2/3 of a classics degree) so I am required to be very analytical. This is a text that means a lot to a lot of people and holds massive cultural significance, so to wholly change parts of the core themes and meanings I feel is unnecessary. There are so many wonderful Greek plays about many of the themes Nolan imposes, so I always wonder in scenarios like this why he didn’t take one more suited. Imposing his own main themes with the result that they actively contradict with core part of the story, I feel, does not result in a good adaptation! (All my opinion I must remind)AGAIN AGAIN, there was a lot about this that I enjoyed, I am delighted that so many people are taking an interest and I sincerely hope that anyone who has watched it and is interested (and if you’ve made it this far through this stream of consciousness), goes and reads the text for themselves! It’s available online for free here on the Project Gutenberg website. Go wild, form your own opinions, and debate. I hope this is coherent.AND, keep reading classical literature!! :DAdditional recommendations (AKA Greek plays I like that you should read while Odyssey mania persists):Oedipus Tyrannus – SophoclesAntigone - SophoclesElektra – EuripidesThe Women of Troy – EuripidesAgamemnon – AeschylusAeneid – Virgil (Latin but the founding story of Rome building off of Homer’s Odyssey following the surviving Trojans on their trip to Troy. A merging of the Iliad and Odyssey with Roman setting)ALSO read the Iliad. Duh.Ok bye.

blocks.html_en: This is not a be-all-end-all review, this is a more critical analysis of the choices made concerning the writing and plot from the lens of criticising an adaptation. There were bits I liked about this film, but I believe some of the choices made detract from the original themes and characters from the text, to the extents that they are genuinely unfaithful at points. Not saying it makes the film unwatchable or awful, but saying that at times, it doesn’t feel like the Odyssey. But hey. Classics students amiright. I will not cover everything in this, because my memory is not good enough for that, but I’ll include as much as I can. This is very stream of consciousness, so I apologise in advance if this makes no sense. Happy to clarify anything that is incoherent lol. SPOILERS ARE ABOUND. IF YOU WANT AN UNSPOILED EXPERIENCE READ OR WATCH FIRST. Ok enjoy.

sentences.en: His trickery at the end also is very watered down.
sentences.zh: 他最后的诡计也被淡化了。

sentences.en: Lack of divine influence results in it just being Matt Damon in a hood rather than an actual disguise.
sentences.zh: 缺乏神圣的影响力导致它只是戴着兜帽的马特·达蒙而不是真正的伪装。

blocks.html_en: • I thought the actual suitor slaughter was actually a little underwhelming? Action hero-ification of Odysseus does not extend to his ability to kill the suitors ig? Again, issue of not having gods (Zeus and Athena help in text, as does Telemachus instead of running around being kind of useless and being told to stay out of it). Severe lack of ruthlessness from Odysseus. Missing the driving force of wanting to return home no matter the cost, no matter the sacrifice. I felt leaving some of the suitors who surrendered alive really detracted from the visceral catharsis and kind of undermined Odysseus’ authority? Like yes these men pursued your wife for years and mocked and intimidated your son and ruined your household but at least they said sorry! The purification of kind of morally grey heroes frustrates me.

sentences.en: I thought the actual suitor slaughter was actually a little underwhelming?
sentences.zh: 我觉得真正的追求者屠杀其实有点平淡？

sentences.en: Action hero-ification of Odysseus does not extend to his ability to kill the suitors ig?
sentences.zh: 奥德修斯的动作英雄化并没有延伸到他杀死IG追求者的能力？

sentences.en: Again, issue of not having gods (Zeus and Athena help in text, as does Telemachus instead of running around being kind of useless and being told to stay out of it).
sentences.zh: 再次，没有神的问题（宙斯和雅典娜在文本中提供帮助，就像特勒马科斯一样，而不是到处乱跑，有点无用，并被告知不要参与其中）。

sentences.en: Severe lack of ruthlessness from Odysseus.
sentences.zh: 奥德修斯严重缺乏冷酷无情。

sentences.en: Missing the driving force of wanting to return home no matter the cost, no matter the sacrifice.
sentences.zh: 失去了无论付出什么代价，无论牺牲什么，想要回家的动力。

sentences.en: I felt leaving some of the suitors who surrendered alive really detracted from the visceral catharsis and kind of undermined Odysseus’ authority?
sentences.zh: 我觉得留下一些活着投降的求婚者真的会减损内心的宣泄，并在某种程度上削弱奥德修斯的权威吗？

sentences.en: Like yes these men pursued your wife for years and mocked and intimidated your son and ruined your household but at least they said sorry!
sentences.zh: 就像是的，这些男人追求你的妻子多年，嘲笑和恐吓你的儿子，毁了你的家庭，但至少他们说对不起！

sentences.en: The purification of kind of morally grey heroes frustrates me.
sentences.zh: 道德上灰色英雄的净化让我感到沮丧。

blocks.html_en: NO GODS

sentences.en: NO GODS
sentences.zh: 没有神

blocks.html_en: • This is dumb for many reasons. Taking a story driven and incited by actions of gods and removing any real evidence of them doesn’t really work, because it then removes credibility from multiple parts of the story.

sentences.en: This is dumb for many reasons.
sentences.zh: 出于多种原因，这很愚蠢。

sentences.en: Taking a story driven and incited by actions of gods and removing any real evidence of them doesn’t really work, because it then removes credibility from multiple parts of the story.
sentences.zh: 接受一个由神的行为驱动和煽动的故事并消除任何真实的证据并没有真正起作用，因为它会消除故事多个部分的可信度。

blocks.html_en: • Nolan wanting to show forces of nature as gods is fun, but ineffective when you don’t actually explain that to the audience as a direct cause!! A one-off mention to Poseidon is hard for those who are unfamiliar with Greek myths to understand! Even more so when the next storm is actually sent by a different god! (Storm after Helios’ island is sent by Zeus. This is not well communicated)

sentences.en: Nolan wanting to show forces of nature as gods is fun, but ineffective when you don’t actually explain that to the audience as a direct cause!!
sentences.zh: 诺兰想要将自然力量展现为神灵很有趣，但当你没有真正向观众解释这一点作为直接原因时，效果就很低了！

sentences.en: A one-off mention to Poseidon is hard for those who are unfamiliar with Greek myths to understand!
sentences.zh: 一次性提到波塞冬对于那些不熟悉希腊神话的人来说是很难理解的！

sentences.en: Even more so when the next storm is actually sent by a different god!
sentences.zh: 更何况，下一场风暴实际上是由不同的神发出的！

sentences.en: (Storm after Helios’ island is sent by Zeus.
sentences.zh: （赫利俄斯岛之后的风暴是宙斯派来的。

sentences.en: This is not well communicated)
sentences.zh: 这个没好好沟通）

blocks.html_en: • Most obvious with the Cyclops, lack of direct and overt address to Poseidon from Polyphemus praying for revenge and for Odysseus to never make it home makes the Greeks seem incompetent in their travels!! The whole point is that no matter how good they are and what they do, because of the insult to Poseidon they will never make it home safely!! They are doomed by Odysseus and are forced to foot his punishment!! Removal of gods removes the interplay between fate and free will, and the notions of godly politics influencing how gods and mortals interact

sentences.en: Most obvious with the Cyclops, lack of direct and overt address to Poseidon from Polyphemus praying for revenge and for Odysseus to never make it home makes the Greeks seem incompetent in their travels!!
sentences.zh: 最明显的是独眼巨人，波吕斐摩斯没有直接和公开地向波塞冬祈祷复仇，也没有让奥德修斯永远回家，这使得希腊人在他们的旅行中显得无能！

sentences.en: The whole point is that no matter how good they are and what they do, because of the insult to Poseidon they will never make it home safely!!
sentences.zh: 重点是，无论他们多么优秀，无论他们做了什么，由于对波塞冬的侮辱，他们永远无法安全回家！

sentences.en: They are doomed by Odysseus and are forced to foot his punishment!!
sentences.zh: 他们被奥德修斯注定了，被迫承受他的惩罚！

sentences.en: Removal of gods removes the interplay between fate and free will, and the notions of godly politics influencing how gods and mortals interact
sentences.zh: 去除诸神消除了命运与自由意志之间的相互作用，以及影响神与凡人互动方式的敬虔政治观念

blocks.html_en: • Additionally, removal of gods forces wisdom imparted by gods (normal, believable as all seeing immortals to be able to act as messengers in this way) to be imparted by random characters. why is Circe randomly giving prophetic advice? Basically, it makes a lot of the exposition seem more forced than it should be.

sentences.en: Additionally, removal of gods forces wisdom imparted by gods (normal, believable as all seeing immortals to be able to act as messengers in this way) to be imparted by random characters.
sentences.zh: 此外，去除神灵会迫使神灵所赋予的智慧（正常，可信，因为所有看到的仙人都能够以这种方式充当使者）由随机角色赋予。

sentences.en: why is Circe randomly giving prophetic advice?
sentences.zh: 为什么喀耳刻会随机给出预言建议？

sentences.en: Basically, it makes a lot of the exposition seem more forced than it should be.
sentences.zh: 基本上，这使得很多阐述看起来比应有的更加强迫。

blocks.html_en: • No Hermes :( crime in and of itself.

sentences.en: No Hermes :( crime in and of itself.
sentences.zh: 没有赫耳墨斯:(犯罪本身。

blocks.html_en: • Also, a lot of the characters whom Athena is disguised as in the text were actually in the film! Everyone Telemachus tells that they have ‘wise eyes. Athena eyes’ is meant to be Athena in the text, but obviously NOT meant to be given they kill Mentor during the assassination. I’m so sad we didn’t get Hermes.

sentences.en: Also, a lot of the characters whom Athena is disguised as in the text were actually in the film!
sentences.zh: 此外，文中雅典娜伪装的很多角色实际上都出现在电影中！

sentences.en: Everyone Telemachus tells that they have ‘wise eyes.
sentences.zh: 每个忒勒马科斯都说他们有“智慧的眼睛”。

sentences.en: Athena eyes’ is meant to be Athena in the text, but obviously NOT meant to be given they kill Mentor during the assassination.
sentences.zh: 雅典娜之眼在文本中本应是雅典娜，但显然不意味着他们在暗杀期间杀死了导师。

sentences.en: I’m so sad we didn’t get Hermes.
sentences.zh: 我很遗憾我们没有得到爱马仕。

blocks.html_en: • The Athena thing was interesting I actually liked that the image taken was that of a girl from Troy, i think the implication is that Odysseus was just hallucinating her?? and she isn’t actually the goddess. She didn’t feel very impactful as a character, though i suppose she’s technically meant to serve as a representation of Odysseus’ guilty conscience. Which again, don’t hate it, but frustrating to build up this concept of Odysseus as a tormented morally pure hero rather than a deceitful trickster willing to do whatever it takes to get home. Idk.

sentences.en: The Athena thing was interesting I actually liked that the image taken was that of a girl from Troy, i think the implication is that Odysseus was just hallucinating her??
sentences.zh: 雅典娜的事情很有趣，我实际上很喜欢这张照片是来自特洛伊的女孩，我认为这意味着奥德修斯只是对她产生了幻觉？

sentences.en: and she isn’t actually the goddess.
sentences.zh: 而她实际上并不是女神。

sentences.en: She didn’t feel very impactful as a character, though i suppose she’s technically meant to serve as a representation of Odysseus’ guilty conscience.
sentences.zh: 作为一个角色，她感觉不太有影响力，尽管我认为从技术上讲她是为了代表奥德修斯的罪恶感。

sentences.en: Which again, don’t hate it, but frustrating to build up this concept of Odysseus as a tormented morally pure hero rather than a deceitful trickster willing to do whatever it takes to get home.
sentences.zh: 再说一次，不要讨厌它，但令人沮丧的是，将奥德修斯塑造成一个饱受折磨的道德纯洁的英雄，而不是一个愿意不惜一切代价回家的欺骗性骗子。

sentences.en: Idk.
sentences.zh: 我不知道。

blocks.html_en: • As a result of lack of divine, try to make excuses for other reasons for Trojan war which, understand why he’s done that, but when the entirety of the war on multiple levels and its perpetuity hinges on the interference and politics of the divines it’s a bit weird. mentions about trade routes and mortal politics to replace the divine, but also inciting incident of the Trojan war is literally bc of gods. Are we ignoring that? Do we care?

sentences.en: As a result of lack of divine, try to make excuses for other reasons for Trojan war which, understand why he’s done that, but when the entirety of the war on multiple levels and its perpetuity hinges on the interference and politics of the divines it’s a bit weird.
sentences.zh: 由于缺乏神圣性，尝试为特洛伊战争寻找其他原因，理解他为什么这样做，但是当整个战争在多个层面上及其永久性取决于神圣者的干预和政治时，这就有点奇怪了。

sentences.en: mentions about trade routes and mortal politics to replace the divine, but also inciting incident of the Trojan war is literally bc of gods.
sentences.zh: 提到贸易路线和凡人政治取代神灵，还煽动特洛伊战争事件，简直就是诸神的公元前。

sentences.en: Are we ignoring that?
sentences.zh: 我们是否忽视了这一点？

sentences.en: Do we care?
sentences.zh: 我们关心吗？

blocks.html_en: • Cherry picking of the divine. Not wanting to actually put in the gods but wanting their effects to come into play without effective communication to audience imo. Like we have Helios’ island (which, did they call him Apollo? I can’t remember but I feel like I crashed out at that point for some reason) which is obviously divine, and there is divine retribution for disobeying the gods, but that comes from Zeus due to divine politics, a layer of vetting of divine punishment that is lost due to lack of direct communication to audience about the gods.

sentences.en: Cherry picking of the divine.
sentences.zh: 神圣的樱桃采摘。

sentences.en: Not wanting to actually put in the gods but wanting their effects to come into play without effective communication to audience imo.
sentences.zh: 我不想真正把诸神放进去，但希望它们的效果在没有与观众有效沟通的情况下发挥作用。

sentences.en: Like we have Helios’ island (which, did they call him Apollo?
sentences.zh: 就像我们有赫利俄斯岛一样（他们称他为阿波罗吗？

sentences.en: I can’t remember but I feel like I crashed out at that point for some reason) which is obviously divine, and there is divine retribution for disobeying the gods, but that comes from Zeus due to divine politics, a layer of vetting of divine punishment that is lost due to lack of direct communication to audience about the gods.
sentences.zh: 我不记得了，但我觉得我当时因为某种原因崩溃了）这显然是神圣的，并且对不服从诸神的神圣报应，但这来自宙斯，由于神圣的政治，一层对神圣惩罚的审查，由于缺乏与观众关于诸神的直接沟通而丢失。

blocks.html_en: • ADDITIONALLY, the whole point of an epic is that the poet is a vessel for the divine. Mortals cannot know the story, so they exhort to the Muses and channel their speech in recounting it. Epics are inherently divine! Why are we scared of leaning into this! Had the suggestion that this was an atheist take on the Odyssey coming from the perspective of reading it with the exclusion of the divine and trying to find the source of the actions which are explained by the divine by the Greeks. I agree this is likely the case, but I feel it’s a disservice to the story to exclude such a major part. That’s not just a differing interpretation, it’s an ignorant at best and wilful at worst exclusion of a massive influence on the plot and the themes imo.

sentences.en: ADDITIONALLY, the whole point of an epic is that the poet is a vessel for the divine.
sentences.zh: 此外，史诗的全部意义在于诗人是神圣的容器。

sentences.en: Mortals cannot know the story, so they exhort to the Muses and channel their speech in recounting it.
sentences.zh: 凡人无法知道这个故事，因此他们劝告缪斯并通过他们的言语来讲述它。

sentences.en: Epics are inherently divine!
sentences.zh: 史诗本质上是神圣的！

sentences.en: Why are we scared of leaning into this!
sentences.zh: 为什么我们害怕陷入这个！

sentences.en: Had the suggestion that this was an atheist take on the Odyssey coming from the perspective of reading it with the exclusion of the divine and trying to find the source of the actions which are explained by the divine by the Greeks.
sentences.zh: 有人认为这是无神论者对《奥德赛》的看法，从排除神的角度来阅读它，并试图找到希腊人用神解释的行为的根源。

sentences.en: I agree this is likely the case, but I feel it’s a disservice to the story to exclude such a major part.
sentences.zh: 我同意情况很可能是这样，但我觉得排除这样一个主要部分对故事来说是一种伤害。

sentences.en: That’s not just a differing interpretation, it’s an ignorant at best and wilful at worst exclusion of a massive influence on the plot and the themes imo.
sentences.zh: 这不仅仅是一种不同的解释，在我看来，这充其量是无知，最坏的是故意排除对情节和主题的巨大影响。

blocks.html_en: • Crash course in the Greek hospitality rules!! ‘ξενια’ - “xenia” – the concept that all mortals should offer help to strangers who come to their homes - food, bathing, a bed for the night - as they could be a god in disguise. As a result, the Greek word for “stranger” – ‘ξενος’ – “xenos”, can also be translated as “guest-friend”. A major theme of the Odyssey is that of hospitality and its violations, and the consequences of such things.

sentences.en: Crash course in the Greek hospitality rules!!
sentences.zh: 希腊待客规则速成班！

sentences.en: ‘ξενια’ - “xenia” – the concept that all mortals should offer help to strangers who come to their homes - food, bathing, a bed for the night - as they could be a god in disguise.
sentences.zh: ‘xiενια’ - “xenia” - 所有凡人都应该为来到他们家中的陌生人提供帮助 - 食物、洗澡、过夜的床 - 因为他们可能是伪装的神。

sentences.en: As a result, the Greek word for “stranger” – ‘ξενος’ – “xenos”, can also be translated as “guest-friend”.
sentences.zh: 因此，希腊语中的“陌生人”一词——“xiενος”——“xenos”，也可以翻译为“客人朋友”。

sentences.en: A major theme of the Odyssey is that of hospitality and its violations, and the consequences of such things.
sentences.zh: 《奥德赛》的一个主要主题是热情好客及其违规行为，以及此类事情的后果。

blocks.html_en: • this is what they referred to as Zeus’ law. It is not actually called that. And they just name dropped it 200000 times without actually taking the time to investigate what it means in context and explain how its violation is significant. Treat others how you want to be treated is a shallow interpretation, and genuine questioning of what it means to give, receive and violate xenia were omitted from a lot of the journey interactions, where these themes hold significance – violations from the Greeks and altogether rejection with Polyphemus, deceitful violations with Circe, overprovision with Calypso, dangers of provision for the Phaeacians. Felt like a very surface level interaction with an absolutely core theme to me

sentences.en: this is what they referred to as Zeus’ law.
sentences.zh: 这就是他们所说的宙斯法则。

sentences.en: It is not actually called that.
sentences.zh: 实际上并不是这样称呼的。

sentences.en: And they just name dropped it 200000 times without actually taking the time to investigate what it means in context and explain how its violation is significant.
sentences.zh: 他们只是点名删除了 200000 次，而没有真正花时间调查它在上下文中的含义，并解释其违规行为的严重性。

sentences.en: Treat others how you want to be treated is a shallow interpretation, and genuine questioning of what it means to give, receive and violate xenia were omitted from a lot of the journey interactions, where these themes hold significance – violations from the Greeks and altogether rejection with Polyphemus, deceitful violations with Circe, overprovision with Calypso, dangers of provision for the Phaeacians.
sentences.zh: 以你希望被对待的方式对待他人是一种肤浅的解释，对给予、接受和侵犯塞尼亚意味着什么的真正质疑在旅程的许多互动中都被省略了，这些主题具有重要意义——希腊人的侵犯和波吕斐摩斯的完全拒绝，喀耳刻的欺骗性侵犯，卡吕普索的过度供给，为斐阿克人提供供给的危险。

sentences.en: Felt like a very surface level interaction with an absolutely core theme to me
sentences.zh: 对我来说，感觉就像是一种非常表面的互动，具有绝对的核心主题

blocks.html_en: Break it down sectional style

sentences.en: Break it down sectional style
sentences.zh: 分解剖面样式

blocks.html_en: • ok so I understand cutting things for time, I am ok with that. my issues were somewhat with what and how things were cut and the amount of attention and time given to certain parts.

sentences.en: ok so I understand cutting things for time, I am ok with that.
sentences.zh: 好吧，我理解为了节省时间而削减事情，我对此表示同意。

sentences.en: my issues were somewhat with what and how things were cut and the amount of attention and time given to certain parts.
sentences.zh: 我的问题有点在于剪辑的内容和方式，以及对某些部分的关注和时间。

blocks.html_en: • I thought the pacing was really weird during the journey sequence. I don’t know why they rushed scenes like the cyclops and Circe, yet gave so much time to other, less significant moments, or additions that don’t necessarily forward the story or the themes.

sentences.en: I thought the pacing was really weird during the journey sequence.
sentences.zh: 我觉得旅途中的节奏真的很奇怪。

sentences.en: I don’t know why they rushed scenes like the cyclops and Circe, yet gave so much time to other, less significant moments, or additions that don’t necessarily forward the story or the themes.
sentences.zh: 我不知道为什么他们匆匆忙忙地拍摄独眼巨人和喀耳刻这样的场景，但却花了这么多时间来处理其他不太重要的时刻，或者不一定能推动故事或主题的补充。

blocks.html_en: • Begins with Telemachus on Ithaca - personally thought this bit went on for too long and without the textual inciting incident (Athena speaking to Telemachus in disguise) it dragged a bit, and Telemachus’ decision to actually do something came a little out of nowhere. I’d have liked less of that at the start (or for it to have been more succinct) and more of the actual journey. Liked the suitors tho!!

sentences.en: Begins with Telemachus on Ithaca - personally thought this bit went on for too long and without the textual inciting incident (Athena speaking to Telemachus in disguise) it dragged a bit, and Telemachus’ decision to actually do something came a little out of nowhere.
sentences.zh: 从伊萨卡岛的特勒马科斯开始——个人认为这一段持续了太长的时间，而且没有文字煽动事件（雅典娜伪装地对特勒马科斯说话），它有点拖沓，特勒马科斯真正做某事的决定有点突然。

sentences.en: I’d have liked less of that at the start (or for it to have been more succinct) and more of the actual journey.
sentences.zh: 我一开始就不喜欢这样（或者更简洁），而喜欢更多实际的旅程。

sentences.en: Liked the suitors tho!!
sentences.zh: 喜欢追求者！

blocks.html_en: • No Nestor!!! My goat I’m so sorry they skipped you.

sentences.en: No Nestor!!!
sentences.zh: 没有内斯特！！！

sentences.en: My goat I’m so sorry they skipped you.
sentences.zh: 我的山羊，我很抱歉他们忽略了你。

blocks.html_en: • THEY SKIPPED HELEN DRUGGING EVERYONE AND I’M MAD ABOUT IT. Liked Helen, liked how vindicative she was, would have liked to see a more complicated, survivalist, playing-each-side vibe to her rather than seeming to just be angry with and hate her husband and her husband hate her. mixed opinions about Menelaus for reasons I can’t really explain. Vibes I guess.

sentences.en: THEY SKIPPED HELEN DRUGGING EVERYONE AND I’M MAD ABOUT IT.
sentences.zh: 他们没有让海伦给每个人下药，我对此很生气。

sentences.en: Liked Helen, liked how vindicative she was, would have liked to see a more complicated, survivalist, playing-each-side vibe to her rather than seeming to just be angry with and hate her husband and her husband hate her.
sentences.zh: 喜欢海伦，喜欢她的报复心，希望看到她有一种更复杂、更生存、互相争斗的氛围，而不是看起来只是对她的丈夫感到愤怒和仇恨，而她的丈夫也讨厌她。

sentences.en: mixed opinions about Menelaus for reasons I can’t really explain.
sentences.zh: 由于我无法真正解释的原因，人们对墨涅拉俄斯的看法褒贬不一。

sentences.en: Vibes I guess.
sentences.zh: 我猜是共鸣。

blocks.html_en: • No Phaeacians :(( In-text, Odysseus washes onshore at Scheria and is taken in by the Phaeacians - initial example of good hospitality to contrast the suitors. Their absence messes with the structure of the story quite a lot, and ruins some of the lovely ring composition :( He tells them the story, not calypso, and they then help him get home and are punished by Poseidon for doing so. (Q of conflict between Xenia and the direct will of the gods). Disregarding the divine makes it hard to include them, but I think they are a more meaningful audience than Calypso - mortal audience rep of the actual listeners of the epic.

sentences.en: No Phaeacians :(( In-text, Odysseus washes onshore at Scheria and is taken in by the Phaeacians - initial example of good hospitality to contrast the suitors.
sentences.zh: 没有Phaeacians :(( 在文本中，奥德修斯在Scheria 冲上岸，并被Phaeacians 收留——这是与求婚者形成鲜明对比的热情好客的第一个例子。

sentences.en: Their absence messes with the structure of the story quite a lot, and ruins some of the lovely ring composition :( He tells them the story, not calypso, and they then help him get home and are punished by Poseidon for doing so.

sentences.en: Disregarding the divine makes it hard to include them, but I think they are a more meaningful audience than Calypso - mortal audience rep of the actual listeners of the epic.

blocks.html_en: • Very much enjoyed the sacking of troy, this is actually my favourite interpretation of the Trojan horse I’ve seen as well, LOVED the half-buried in sand vibe and the literal drowning with the tide. Based. Whole invasion sequence was also fun. Liked the detail of the Trojans using the rolling logs to move it too. HOWEVER. For a film whose new and added theme is the horror of war, it felt very unhorrible? The barbarism of the Greeks at times felt watered down, and I felt Nolan shied away from the true crimes that were committed (assaults, taking slaves etc) which I think ties back to this puritan image of a troubled soldier that he wants Odysseus to represent. YES, he’s TROUBLED by WAR but he didn‘t do too many bad things so he’s redeemable!! Odysseus is kind of a bastard in the text and everyone knows it! Lean into that and I assure you you’ll get better internal conflict than “I don’t want to go home” from guy whose entire purpose in this story is seeking home.

blocks.html_en: • Making Polyphemus an ineloquent/unintelligent creature rather than a speaking, divine adjacent being undermines that whole fight imo. It’s man vs monster not man vs enemy (if that makes sense??) Odysseus doesn’t have to outsmart an intelligent being, but a more animalistic creature. Taking away that intelligence reduces him to another monster of the week instead of the opponent who understands Odysseus’ intentions and offer of peace and instead makes the explicit choice of violence - shows a different sort of danger. Asking Odysseus’ name in what seems to be an acceptance of hospitality then turning around to state that he will kill him last. Absolute cinema.

sentences.en: Making Polyphemus an ineloquent/unintelligent creature rather than a speaking, divine adjacent being undermines that whole fight imo.
sentences.zh: 在我看来，让波吕斐摩斯成为一个不善言辞/不聪明的生物，而不是一个会说话的、神圣的邻近生物，破坏了整个战斗。

blocks.html_en: • Additionally, losing the speech results in no direct prayer to Poseidon (additional issue of lack of direct divinity) – as a result, 1 line of exposition revealing Polyphemus as son of Poseidon and a vague implication that this will be bad. Direct prayer is so key to original as it is the moment that Odysseus’ arrogance, intelligence and hubris dooms himself and his crew to their fates. His need to outsmart and gloat over his opponent causes Polyphemus to call directly for retribution, praying that Odysseus will never make it home, or that if he does he will be late, without honour and following the death of his whole crew. Key moment of damnation becomes vague and unclear, the tragic doomed narrative is lost as we don’t have the divine confirmation that every attempt Odysseus makes to fight fate and save his crew is in vain. Small details have important narrative consequence.

sentences.en: Additionally, losing the speech results in no direct prayer to Poseidon (additional issue of lack of direct divinity) – as a result, 1 line of exposition revealing Polyphemus as son of Poseidon and a vague implication that this will be bad.
sentences.zh: 此外，失去演讲会导致无法直接向波塞冬祈祷（缺乏直接神性的额外问题）——因此，第一行解释揭示波利斐摩斯是波塞冬的儿子，并含糊地暗示这会很糟糕。

sentences.en: Direct prayer is so key to original as it is the moment that Odysseus’ arrogance, intelligence and hubris dooms himself and his crew to their fates.
sentences.zh: 直接祈祷对于原创至关重要，因为正是在这一时刻，奥德修斯的傲慢、智慧和傲慢注定了他自己和他的船员的命运。

sentences.en: His need to outsmart and gloat over his opponent causes Polyphemus to call directly for retribution, praying that Odysseus will never make it home, or that if he does he will be late, without honour and following the death of his whole crew.
sentences.zh: 他需要智取对手并幸灾乐祸，这导致波吕斐摩斯直接要求报应，祈祷奥德修斯永远不会回家，或者如果他回家了，他就会迟到，没有荣誉，并且在他的全体船员都死后。

sentences.en: Key moment of damnation becomes vague and unclear, the tragic doomed narrative is lost as we don’t have the divine confirmation that every attempt Odysseus makes to fight fate and save his crew is in vain.
sentences.zh: 诅咒的关键时刻变得模糊不清，注定的悲剧叙事消失了，因为我们没有神圣的确认，奥德修斯为对抗命运和拯救他的船员所做的每一次尝试都是徒劳的。

sentences.en: Small details have important narrative consequence.
sentences.zh: 小细节具有重要的叙事后果。

blocks.html_en: • I have opinions about Nolan’s writing of female characters. You’ve given calypso more screen time only for her to just. Prompt Odysseus. Why. Use that time to investigate godhood and its (in)compatibility with mortality. Or something more than being a prompter for a man. Idk. I would’ve liked to see more of Circe being powerful and manipulative and vicious towards the men (yes, even more than we got) and less of Circe immediately folding to Odysseus after he asks nicely. blocks.html_en: • I felt very much that the majority of trying to make “strong female characters” resulted in making Penelope be angry and shout and hit us with classic lines like “women can’t take the throne!!” Which, yes, point. However, that’s not really how Penelope approached it?? Penelope and Odysseus work because they are equally as tricksy and intelligent as each other. They brought in the weaving trick (which in the text is already concluded) so I would’ve loved to see that represented rather than expositioned at us! Show me Penelope weaving all day, then tirelessly unweaving it by candlelight all night. Show me her plotting more subtle ways to delay the suitors instead of just shouting empty words! I don’t mind extrapolation to improve a character, but align it with their actual characterisation!

blocks.html_en: • And to boot, we absolutely ignore the best scene for Penelope which proves her intellectual equality to Odysseus - the olive bed scene!!! Post suitor slaughter, Penelope does not trust that this man is Odysseus (the pin does not exist. Done for cinematic ease I presume. Maybe if we didn’t spend so much time on boats we’d have had time for the DEFINING MOMENT OF THEIR RELATIONSHIP IN THE TEXT.) She tells him to move their bed in their room, a final test for Odysseus to prove himself, as their wedding bed was carved by Odysseus, with one post being a live olive tree – room built around the tree. Him pointing this out proves his identity, and shows Penelope’s loyalty, dedication and intellect at the same time, withholding her relief until she is absolutely certain her husband is home.

blocks.html_en: • I appreciate attempts to give Penelope more agency but her entire arc is founded on her inability to take direct action. Subtle actions are her strength, and some elements which give thematic and societal information are also excluded – the veil is one I noticed (in some ways replaced by the screen wall and door, but lacks the societal implications). High born women wore veils when in public and had to be accompanied by chaperones – the interesting part is that Penelope begins to wear a veil in her house when speaking to the suitors, and be accompanied by two of her maids at all times – implication is that the invasion of the suitors has destroyed the notion of home for Penelope, forcing her to take outside precautions in her own household.

Home and homecoming is core to Odysseus’ motivations, and the way that homes are built and honoured and violated and destroyed is key to this text.
